name: AutoGen (Microsoft)
description: AutoGen is Microsoft Research's open-source framework for building multi-agent AI applications. In April 2026, Microsoft shipped Agent Framework 1.0 — the production-ready evolution combining Semantic Kernel foundations with AutoGen orchestration, offering enterprise-grade multi-agent orchestration, multi-provider model support, and cross-runtime interoperability via A2A and MCP.
oneLiner: Microsoft Research's multi-agent conversation framework with Azure enterprise integration and visual Studio UI.
funding: Backed by Microsoft Research. No external VC — fully Microsoft-funded.
pricing: Open Source (MIT, free); Azure AI Agent Service (consumption-based); Copilot Studio ($200/user/mo)

screenshots.alt: AutoGen Studio — agent configuration with role and model settings
AutoGen Studio — multi-agent workflow session with conversation view
AutoGen Studio — drag-and-drop canvas for agent skill assignment
AutoGen Studio — agent execution profiling and performance visualization

capabilities: Multi-agent conversations (group chat, nested, sequential, swarm)

uxAnalysis.builderFlow: Code-first Python: define agents with system messages, LLM configs, tool registrations, compose into conversations. AutoGen Studio provides browser-based visual builder for drag-and-drop prototyping. 0.4 rewrite introduced AgentChat high-level API and Core runtime. Enterprise users build through Azure AI Agent Service or Copilot Studio.
strengths: Flexible topologies — group chat, sequential, nested, swarm
weaknesses: No production monitoring or cost tracking in OSS
AutoGen Studio is prototyping, not production
No RBAC or compliance in OSS
Steep learning curve for advanced patterns
notableFeatures: Magentic-One: multi-agent team (Orchestrator, WebSurfer, FileSurfer, Coder, ComputerTerminal)
AutoGen Studio: visual builder for multi-agent workflows
Azure AI Agent Service bridge to enterprise

recentDevelopments: 2026-04: Microsoft Agent Framework 1.0 shipped — production-ready release merging Semantic Kernel + AutoGen with stable APIs for .NET and Python
2026-04: Enterprise-grade multi-agent orchestration with A2A and MCP cross-runtime interoperability
2025-10: Microsoft announced combining Semantic Kernel + AutoGen into unified Microsoft Agent Framework
2025-01: AutoGen v0.4 major rewrite — reimagined for scale with new architecture
2025: AutoGen Studio v0.4 with drag-and-drop multi-agent builder, real-time updates, flow visualizations

deploymentOptions.notes: Open-source Python framework from Microsoft Research. Self-hosted. AutoGen Studio provides local web UI.

subCriteria.name: Agent Builder UX
subCriteria.rationale: Code-first Python SDK. 0.4 improved API but breaking changes. Studio for visual prototyping.

subCriteria.name: Time-to-Value
subCriteria.rationale: Studio visualizes conversations and tools. AutoGen Bench for evaluation. Interactive debugging limited.

subCriteria.name: Debugging & Testing
subCriteria.rationale: Comprehensive with notebooks. 0.2-to-0.4 transition created parallel doc confusion.

subCriteria.name: Documentation & Learning
subCriteria.rationale: Studio enables rapid prototyping. Code changes require restart. No hot-reload.

subCriteria.name: Multi-Agent Coordination
subCriteria.rationale: Category-defining. Group chat, two-agent, sequential, nested, swarm, Magentic-One. Dynamic spawning.

subCriteria.name: Human-in-the-Loop Design
subCriteria.rationale: UserProxyAgent is first-class participant. Natural handoff. Configurable intervention.

subCriteria.name: Workflow Templates
subCriteria.rationale: No collaborative features in OSS. Studio is single-user.

subCriteria.name: Team Development
subCriteria.rationale: Notebooks as examples. Studio has samples. No template marketplace.

subCriteria.name: Native Connectors
subCriteria.rationale: Limited pre-built connectors. Azure unlocks Microsoft ecosystem.

subCriteria.name: API & Protocol Standards
subCriteria.rationale: OpenAI function calling. Tool use patterns. Early MCP exploration. OpenAPI via Azure.

subCriteria.name: Authentication & Access
subCriteria.rationale: Basic in OSS. Azure AD and managed identity via Azure AI Agent Service.

subCriteria.name: Data Pipeline & RAG
subCriteria.rationale: Basic RAG agent pattern. No sophisticated data pipeline or knowledge management.

subCriteria.name: Guardrails & Kill Switches
subCriteria.rationale: Configurable termination and max rounds. Docker sandboxing. No ML-powered guardrails.

subCriteria.name: Audit Trail & Compliance
subCriteria.rationale: UserProxyAgent provides native human approval. One of strongest HITL in OSS.

subCriteria.name: RBAC & Access Control
subCriteria.rationale: Conversation logs but no tamper-proof storage. Azure inherits compliance.

subCriteria.name: Explainability & Transparency
subCriteria.rationale: No RBAC in OSS. Studio has basic user management. Enterprise needs Azure.

subCriteria.name: Deployment Flexibility
subCriteria.rationale: Runs anywhere Python does. Azure for managed cloud. Docker support. No K8s operator.

subCriteria.name: Scaling & Monitoring
subCriteria.rationale: 0.4 async supports concurrent agents. Azure handles scaling. OSS is single-process.

subCriteria.name: Cost Management
subCriteria.rationale: Studio has basic visualization. Azure Monitor available. No OSS production monitoring.

subCriteria.name: Ecosystem Maturity
subCriteria.rationale: ~38K GitHub stars, 450+ contributors. Large Discord. Academic citations. 0.4 rewrite fragmented community.

scoringRationale: 62/100 overall. Strongest: Orchestration (14/20). Weakest: Governance (11/20). Breakdown: Creation (12/20), Orchestration (14/20), Integration (12/20), Governance (11/20), Operations (13/20).
targetUser: Researchers and developers building conversational multi-agent systems

communityBuzz: Reports of Microsoft shifting AutoGen to maintenance mode in favor of broader Agent Framework
0.2 to 0.4 rewrite fragmented the community — parallel docs causing confusion
Magentic-One gets attention but AutoGen Studio seen as prototyping tool, not production-ready
UserProxyAgent HITL pattern widely praised as best-in-class for human-agent collaboration
Azure AI Agent Service bridge seen as the real enterprise play

metrics.value: Reports of shift to maintenance mode

walkthrough.title: AutoGen (Microsoft Agent Framework): PM & CTO Walkthrough

pmVerdict.summary: AutoGen has been reborn as the Microsoft Agent Framework (MAF) — merging AutoGen + Semantic Kernel into a unified, production-ready platform. The KodeKloud lab environment provides structured learning with 5 progressive tasks. Memory (SQLite), streaming, and runtime configuration are well-designed. But the rebrand creates confusion, the framework is .NET/Python-focused, and the community is fragmented between old AutoGen and new MAF.
strengths: Microsoft backing provides enterprise credibility and long-term commitment
KodeKloud lab environment — best structured learning experience in the space
SQLite memory pattern is simple and effective for development
Progressive task structure (5 labs) makes learning systematic
risks: AutoGen → MAF rebrand creates confusion — which docs apply?
Community fragmented between old AutoGen and new MAF
Competing with LangGraph and CrewAI with less ecosystem momentum

ctoVerdict.summary: Microsoft Agent Framework 1.0 merges AutoGen + Semantic Kernel — a bet on Microsoft's enterprise distribution. The framework is technically sound (async agents, structured output, runtime options) but the rebrand fractures the community and documentation. If you're a .NET/Microsoft shop, MAF is the natural choice. If you're Python-first, LangGraph has stronger ecosystem momentum.
strengths: Microsoft enterprise distribution (Azure, M365, Teams integration)
Semantic Kernel integration for .NET enterprise developers
Structured learning via KodeKloud — fastest onboarding for new teams
Production-ready with async patterns and streaming built in
dealBreakers: Rebrand fragmentation — AutoGen docs ≠ MAF docs
Community momentum behind LangGraph and CrewAI, not MAF

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Agent creation with role/goal/backstory shown in Jupyter and lab environments
scoringAlignment.gap: AutoGen Studio (visual UI) not demonstrated in this video

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Multi-agent patterns described, task context chaining shown
scoringAlignment.gap: Complex multi-agent orchestration not demonstrated live

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: OpenAI ChatClient, tool creation, API integration patterns shown

scoringAlignment.gap: Governance score relies on Azure platform, not demonstrated

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Runtime options, streaming, memory shown. Deployment not shown
scoringAlignment.gap: No production deployment or monitoring demonstrated

steps.title: Microsoft Agent Framework
steps.description: Build intelligent AI agents with Python — complete course overview of Microsoft Agent Framework (formerly AutoGen).

works: Clear course structure — from basics to production patterns
Microsoft branding provides enterprise credibility
Python-focused tutorial appeals to data science and ML teams
doesntWork: Title still says 'AutoGen' in some places — rebrand inconsistency
Course is 1.5 hours — significant time commitment for evaluation
No quick-start for developers who just want to try it
pmCritique.userPsychology: The rebrand from AutoGen to Microsoft Agent Framework signals enterprise ambition. But the naming confusion means developers search for 'AutoGen' and find outdated docs.

ctoCritique.verdict: Microsoft backing is a strong enterprise signal. But the AutoGen → MAF transition means documentation, community answers, and examples may reference the old API.
concerns: Documentation fragmentation between AutoGen and MAF
Community confusion about which version to use
Migration guide quality unknown
enterpriseGaps: No clear deprecation timeline for old AutoGen API
No compatibility layer between AutoGen and MAF
Enterprise support SLA unclear for MAF vs Azure AI

steps.title: Agent Memory System
steps.description: SQLite-backed memory: save_memory and get_memory functions. Key-value store with timestamp for persistence across conversations.

works: SQLite memory is simple, portable, and zero-infrastructure
Key-value pattern is universally understood
Timestamp-based retrieval enables temporal queries
doesntWork: SQLite isn't production-grade for concurrent agents
No memory isolation between agents — shared database
No memory size limits or cleanup policy
pmCritique.userPsychology: SQLite memory is the right choice for tutorials and prototyping. Production needs Redis or Postgres, but the pattern transfer is clean.

ctoCritique.verdict: SQLite for development, Cosmos DB/Redis for production — the pattern is good but the migration path isn't shown. How do we switch backends without rewriting memory code?
concerns: SQLite doesn't scale for concurrent agent access
No memory backend abstraction — coupled to SQLite
No memory encryption or access control

steps.title: Build a Simple Agent
steps.description: Scenario: smart flight planner widget. User says 'Hi, my name is John Doe' — agent must understand, remember, and plan.

works: Practical scenario — flight planning is relatable and concrete
User message shows natural language understanding requirement
Name extraction demonstrates memory + NLU together
doesntWork: Single-sentence scenario is oversimplified
No error case shown — what if input is ambiguous?
Flight planning requires API integration not shown yet
pmCritique.userPsychology: Starting with a relatable scenario (flight planning) is smart. Every developer has booked a flight and can imagine the agent's job. Concrete beats abstract.

ctoCritique.verdict: The scenario is clear but toy-scale. Enterprise evaluation needs: multi-turn conversation, error recovery, concurrent users, and API integration. This is step 1 of 100.
concerns: Toy scenario doesn't demonstrate enterprise complexity
No multi-turn conversation shown
No concurrent user handling

steps.title: KodeKloud Lab Environment
steps.description: Structured lab: 5 progressive tasks (verify env, first agent, streaming, customize, runtime). Mission: Build Your First AI Agent with MAF. 45-60 minutes.

works: Progressive task structure — each lab builds on the previous
Time estimate (45-60 min) sets realistic expectations
Lab environment means no local setup needed — browser-only
doesntWork: KodeKloud is a paid platform — not accessible to all evaluators
Lab environment may not match production Python environments
5 tasks cover basics only — no advanced patterns
pmCritique.userPsychology: KodeKloud is the best structured learning experience in the agent framework space. No other framework offers a browser-based lab with progressive tasks. This should be the model.

ctoCritique.verdict: Browser-based labs eliminate setup friction. But the lab environment is KodeKloud's, not Microsoft's — the experience doesn't transfer to production Azure environments.
concerns: KodeKloud lab ≠ production Azure environment
Lab tasks are prescriptive — no room for exploration
Lab completion doesn't mean production readiness
enterpriseGaps: No enterprise training program for MAF

steps.title: Create First Agent
steps.description: task_1_first_agent.py: OpenAI ChatClient setup with system message. File structure shows 5 progressive task files.

works: Clean code structure — imports, client, system message, run
5 task files visible in explorer — learning progression is explicit
OpenAI ChatClient is familiar to most developers
doesntWork: Tied to OpenAI — Azure OpenAI not shown as default
System message is the entire agent 'personality' — no structured config
No agent testing or validation step
pmCritique.userPsychology: The first agent file is clean and approachable. But 'first agent = ChatClient with system message' is identical to a basic OpenAI script. Where's the framework value?

ctoCritique.verdict: The first agent is essentially a ChatCompletion wrapper. Framework value comes in tasks 3-5. Enterprise evaluators should skip to task 4 for a realistic assessment.
concerns: First agent doesn't demonstrate framework value
OpenAI dependency in a Microsoft framework is confusing
No Azure AI integration shown
enterpriseGaps: Should default to Azure OpenAI for Microsoft alignment
No model governance or approved model list
No cost estimation per agent run

steps.title: Streaming Responses
steps.description: Real-time token streaming with async agent execution. Stream tokens as they're generated for responsive UX.

works: Async streaming pattern — production-ready for real-time applications
Token-by-token output enables responsive UI
Standard async/await pattern — idiomatic Python
doesntWork: Streaming adds complexity — error handling during streams is tricky
No backpressure handling shown
UI integration for streaming not demonstrated
pmCritique.userPsychology: Streaming is a production requirement, not a feature. The fact that it's task 3 (not an afterthought) shows MAF thinks about production patterns early.

ctoCritique.verdict: Async streaming is well-implemented. But production streaming needs: connection management, partial response recovery, and client-side buffering. The basics are solid.
concerns: No streaming error recovery
No connection timeout handling
No partial response management

steps.title: Runtime Configuration
steps.description: additional_instructions for response control: length, audience adaptation, format. Experiment-driven agent customization.

works: additional_instructions enables runtime behavior modification
Audience adaptation (brief vs detailed) — practical for multi-audience agents
Experiment framing encourages iterative development
doesntWork: additional_instructions is prompt injection by design — security concern
No structured configuration — free-text modification of behavior
No A/B testing framework — manual experimentation only
pmCritique.userPsychology: Runtime modification via additional_instructions is flexible but dangerous. In production, 'anyone can change agent behavior with a string' is a governance nightmare.

ctoCritique.verdict: additional_instructions enables per-request customization. But in enterprise, who controls what instructions are allowed? This needs a policy layer.
concerns: additional_instructions is unvalidated prompt injection
No instruction approval workflow
No instruction audit logging
enterpriseGaps: Must add instruction validation for production

steps.title: Multi-Agent Teams
steps.description: Agent coordination with Semantic Kernel integration for enterprise. Planning, execution, and result aggregation across agents.

works: Semantic Kernel integration bridges .NET and Python enterprise developers
Multi-agent coordination enables complex workflow automation
Microsoft ecosystem integration (Azure, M365) is natural
doesntWork: Multi-agent patterns described but not demonstrated in this frame
Semantic Kernel adds another framework to learn
No visual representation of agent team topology
pmCritique.userPsychology: Multi-agent teams are MAF's enterprise pitch — specialized agents coordinated by a supervisor. The Semantic Kernel bridge is unique to Microsoft.

ctoCritique.verdict: Semantic Kernel integration is the enterprise differentiator — .NET teams can participate in agent development alongside Python teams. No other framework offers this.
concerns: Multi-agent demo not shown — conceptual only
Semantic Kernel learning curve on top of MAF
.NET + Python interop complexity
enterpriseGaps: No cost attribution per agent in a team

youtubeReviews.title: Agentic AI With Autogen Crash Course (4hr)

coreCompetence.bestAt: Most research-advanced multi-agent conversation framework — pioneered the conversable agent pattern where agents debate, critique, and iterate to produce better outputs.
coreCompetence.targetUser: ML researchers and advanced developers exploring multi-agent conversation patterns. Teams building agents that need to reason through complex problems via debate.
coreCompetence.vsAlternatives: Wins against CrewAI on multi-agent conversation depth and research novelty (AutoGen Studio). Wins against LangGraph on pure research flexibility. Loses to both on production deployment tooling. Loses to enterprise platforms on governance and monitoring.
